medianHouseholdIncome.label: Median household income
medianHouseholdIncome.source: Census ACS 5-year (via Data USA / Census Reporter)
medianHouseholdIncome.note: County-wide figure blends struggling post-industrial cities (Gary, East Chicago) with affluent suburbs (St. John, Munster, Schererville), so the median masks a wide internal spread.

medianHomeValue.label: Median home value
medianHomeValue.note: County median owner-occupied value; suburban towns run far above it while values in parts of Gary and East Chicago are a fraction of the county figure.

metrics.label: Bridges in poor condition
metrics.source: FHWA National Bridge Inventory / ARTBA (Indiana profile)
metrics.note: Indiana statewide is ~5.3% structurally deficient; Lake County's older industrial spans, heavy truck traffic and deferred maintenance in Gary/East Chicago push the county estimate above the state rate.

metrics.label: Roads in poor ride quality
metrics.note: Reflects aging local street networks in the northern industrial cities alongside better-maintained suburban arterials; county-wide blend estimated above the Indiana average.

metrics.label: Roundabout ratio
metrics.note: 79 roundabout ways vs 897 signalized-intersection nodes in OpenStreetMap; reported as an adoption index (shrunk toward zero for low counts).

metrics.label: Traffic deaths
metrics.note: Near the Indiana county average; heavy interstate freight corridors (I-80/94, I-65, I-90) elevate exposure.

metrics.label: Sidewalk coverage
metrics.note: Dense older cities (Hammond, Gary core, East Chicago) retain legacy sidewalk grids; postwar suburbs are patchier. OSM sidewalk mapping is incomplete, so this understates true coverage.

metrics.label: Park acres per 1,000 residents
metrics.source: Trust for Public Land / PAD-US / local parks (modeled)
metrics.note: Lakefront and dunes access (Indiana Dunes NP and state parks lie just east in Porter/LaPorte) plus county and municipal parks give a moderate figure, though maintenance in the older cities is uneven.

metrics.label: Residents within a 10-min walk of a park
metrics.note: Higher in the compact older cities, lower across low-density suburban tracts; county-wide blend estimated near the U.S. average.

metrics.label: Playground quality
metrics.note: Suburban parks are newer and well-kept; playgrounds in disinvested Gary/East Chicago neighborhoods show aging equipment and deferred maintenance, pulling the county blend down.

metrics.label: Library visits per capita
metrics.note: Lake County has a strong library tradition (Lake County Public Library is Indiana's 3rd largest, plus several independent municipal districts). Estimated near the Indiana average.

metrics.label: Tree canopy
metrics.note: Suburban and residential canopy is decent, but the heavily industrialized lakefront corridor (steel mills, refineries, rail) holds large low-canopy areas that lower the county average.

metrics.label: Learning rate
metrics.note: County blend: strong suburban districts (Munster, Crown Point, Lake Central) near or above one grade/year offset weaker growth in high-poverty urban districts (Gary, East Chicago).

metrics.label: Grade levels vs. national average
metrics.note: County-wide average sits below the national mean, dragged by low-income urban districts even as affluent suburban schools test above the national average.

metrics.label: Bachelor's degree or higher (age 25+)
metrics.note: Roughly 90% of Indiana's statewide attainment rate; higher in the suburbs, markedly lower in the older industrial cities.

metrics.label: Life expectancy
metrics.note: Roughly 3-4 years below the national average; Lake County ranks around 50th of Indiana's 92 counties on health outcomes, with far shorter life expectancy in the disinvested urban core than in the suburbs.

metrics.label: Child wellbeing index
metrics.source: modeled from CDC PLACES / KIDS COUNT
metrics.note: High child-poverty concentrations in Gary and East Chicago (compounded by lead exposure) pull the county index below average despite better outcomes in suburban towns.

metrics.label: Violent crime
metrics.source: FBI Crime Data Explorer (modeled county blend)
metrics.note: Gary's violent-crime rate is very high (~9 per 1,000), but county-wide the rate is much lower because most residents live in safe suburbs; this figure is a population-weighted county estimate.

metrics.label: Drinking-water violation severity
metrics.note: Serious legacy lead problem: an estimated two-thirds of Indiana's ~55,000 lead service lines (~36,000) are in Northwest Indiana; Gary is replacing ~4,000 lines and East Chicago has ~3,400 remaining lead lines around a Superfund site. Suburban systems are far cleaner, so the county blend is elevated but not maximal.

metrics.label: Broadband availability (100/20 Mbps)
metrics.note: As a mostly urbanized county, cable and fixed-wireless coverage (fixed wireless alone ~98.8%) put nearly all residents within the 100/20 tier; the gap here is affordability and adoption, not availability.

metrics.label: Gigabit availability
metrics.note: Fiber reaches ~43% of the county (5 residential fiber providers); gigabit-capable cable extends this in built-up areas, estimated ~40% overall.

metrics.label: National Register listings per 10,000 residents
metrics.note: ~84 NRHP properties and districts (Lake County Courthouse, Marktown Historic District, Gary's planned-city districts like Horace Mann and Morningside). A genuine, deep historic fabric — much of it inherited from the industrial boom and now under-maintained.

metrics.label: Cultural venues per 10,000 residents
metrics.note: Includes theaters, museums and venues (e.g. Hard Rock Casino entertainment venue, county historical sites, the Genesis Convention Center legacy). Estimated near the small-metro average.

metrics.label: Walkable town center quality
metrics.note: Wide internal spread: Crown Point's courthouse square and downtown Hammond/Whiting offer real walkable centers, while downtown Gary — once a full commercial core — is largely vacant. County blend is middling.

metrics.label: Annual capital outlay per resident
metrics.note: County-wide blend is modest: growing suburbs invest in new capital, but fiscally strained cities like Gary and East Chicago spend little on new capital relative to their large inherited asset base.

metrics.label: Net capital assets per resident
metrics.note: The classic inherited-capital story: cities like Gary hold large stocks of streets, water mains, schools and civic buildings built for a peak population they no longer have, and can no longer fully maintain — high book stock, falling real value.

metrics.label: Active investment trend
metrics.note: Mixed: real disinvestment and population loss in the industrial cities, partly offset by federally funded catch-up (lead-line replacement, brownfield remediation) and active growth/investment in the southern suburbs. Net trend is below average but not collapsing.
